titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Ellison and Lottie Hildreth Papers
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Martha Lund Smalley

controlnote.p: This encoded findingaid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

conventiondeclaration.abbr: finding aid prepared according to local divinity library descriptive practices.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday September 2, 2020 at 22:07

did.abstract: The papers provide valuable documentation of the work of the American Baptist South China Mission, complementing the Foster Family papers and Campbell Family papers also located at Yale Divinity School Library. The Hildreths' experiences are representative of many American missionaries to China in the early part of this century. Substantive correspondence with family members and fellow missionaries documents their striving to reach the mission field, their initial impressions of China, family life in China, daily educational and evangelistic work, and the political unrest rampant in South China during their tenure. The intense anti-Christian movement in the mid 1920s is well documented. Ellison Hildreth served as a Baptist home missionary from 1910 until 1913. From 1913-1927, the Hildreths were American Baptist missionaries in South China. During a furlough from 1918-1919, Ellison Hildreth served with the YMCA in Siberia. From 1928-1949, he served as pastor of Federated Churches in Vermont and Connecticut.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Ellison and Lottie Hildreth Papers (RG 15), Special Collections, Yale Divinity School Library.

chronitemset.event: Lottie Rowe Lane born in Walpole, Massachusetts, daughter of Everett Lane and Carrie Wetherby Lane.
Ellison Story Hildreth born in Holyoke, Massachusetts, son of Judge John Hildreth and Kate Barker Story Hildreth. (Ellison was also known as "Bob", and in later life as E. Story.)
Lottie, B.A., Mt. Holyoke College. Following graduation, she taught school in New York and New Jersey.
Ellison graduated from Union Theological Seminary, N.Y.
Ellison became a Student Volunteer at the SVM convention in Rochester, N.Y.
Ellison taught at Cedarcroft School, Pennsylvania.
Ellison served as Baptist home missionary in Covelo, California.
Lottie and Ellison married. Children: John Lane Hildreth and Alice Lane Hildreth.

American Baptist missionaries in South China. After two years of language study in Kakchieh, they were assigned to Chaochaofu. On return from furlough in 1920, Ellison was in charge of the country work around Chaochaofu and in 1923 a few churches north of Shantou (Swatow) were added to his field.
Ellison served with YMCA in Siberia.
Ellison attended seminary. Family unable to return to China because of poor health.
Ellison pastor of Federated Church in Cabot, Vermont.
Ellison pastor of Federated Church in South Royalton, Vermont.
Ellison pastor of Federated Church of Christ, Brooklyn, Connecticut.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Hildreth Papers provide valuable documentation of the work of the American Baptist South China mission, complementing the Foster Family Papers and Campbell Family Papers also located at YDSL. Ellison and Lottie Hildreth were well-educated and intelligent New Englanders whose experiences are representative of many American missionaries to China in the early part of this century.

Substantive correspondence with family members and fellow missionaries documents their striving to reach the mission field, their initial impressions of China, family life in China (including the death in infancy of their first two children), daily educational and evangelistic work. and the political unrest rampant in South China during their tenure. Extensive correspondence from Ellison to Lottie during the six years prior to their marriage deals primarily with events of daily life but provides glimpses of the difficulties encountered in coordinating family expectations with early plans to go to China. In letters from China to their parents, Ellison and Lottie give detailed descriptions of events and surroundings. In April of 1916. for example, Ellison wrote to his mother about the revolution taking place around them: "Swatow has gracefully gone to the rebels. The chief magistrate fled on board a Chinese warship and left the harbor. We hear that Chaoyang, Kityang, Unkung, Thenghai and Kaying have all sided with the rebels. That means that the power and the soldiers are all in the hands of the revolutionary party, and we can expect as good protection as we have had or better." (ESH to mother 1916 Apr 1 (Box 3, Folder 34))

During this period of unrest, Ellison and some fellow missionaries were involved in negotiations between warring factions. (A rubbing of the granite stone memorializing this involvement is in OVERSIZE material. Photograph and translation available in Box 11, Folder 148.)The intense anti-Christian movement in the mid 1920s is well documented. A letter from Ellison to William Ashmore and others dated 1925 Dec 28, for example, provides detailed description of anti-Christian demonstrations occurring at Christmas in Chaochaofu.

Substantive documentation of the situation in South China is also available following the Hildreths' final return to the U.S. in 1927 through letters of fellow missionaries, particularly those of Ben and Alice Baker, the Hildreth's American Baptist colleagues at Chaochaofu.

The Notes and Writings of Series II deal primarily with the China experiences of the Hildreths. Included are many notes for talks about China given after their return to the United States. Of particular interest among the writings about China collected by the Hildreths is a typescript document by Emanuel H. Giedt entitled "Early Mission History of the Swatow Region Brought Down to the Present for the American Baptist Mission" (1946).

The Personal Items and Memorabilia of Series III shed light on the context of the Hildreths' China years. Family background is provided through newspaper clippings and an autobiographical sketch of Ellison's early years. A notebook-diary from the China years details daily events. A few written musings of Lottie provide insight into her personal feelings and beliefs. Photographs of the Hildreths' surroundings and colleagues in China are well identified.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Place names were modernized in the description, with the name originally used in the collection material or in an older version of the finding aid in parenthesis: e.g. "Beijing (Peking)" or "Benin (Dahomey)".

corpname.part: Young Men's Christian Associations of China

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series is divided into the following categories:

list.item: 1. Correspondence of Ellison Story Hildreth (to LLH, to/from parents)
2. Correspondence of Lottie Lane Hildreth (to ESH, to/from parents)
3. Correspondence of Ellison and Lottie Hildreth (to/from children, siblings, aunt)

did.unittitle: ESH to Lottie Lane Hildreth, from China and the United States
ESH to John and Kate Barker Story Hildreth (parents)
ESH from John and Kate Barker Story Hildreth (parents)
LLH to Carrie and Everett Lane (parents)
LLH to Carrie and Everett Lane (parents)
Correspondence of Ellison and Lottie Hildreth
ESH and LLH to/from Lucelia Wetherby (aunt of LLH)
ESH and LLH to/from Fannie Hildreth (sister of ESH)
ESH and LLH to/from John (Jay) Hildreth (son)
ESH and LLH to/from Alice Hildreth Ballou and family (daughter)
ESH and LLH to/from siblings of LLH
ESH and LLH to/from siblings of LLH
Associate Mission Treasurers (See also: Lacey, Ethel)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series is divided into the following categories:
The notes and writings are arranged chronologically within these categories.

did.unittitle: Thesis submitted to Rochester Theological Seminary: "The Present Apparent Conflict between Foreign and Nationalist Interests in China, with a Suggested Plan of Reconciliation"
"Kakchieh Weakly News", Vol. I, Nos. 1 and 2
Including abstract of letter re: Baptist Central China Mission (1914) and "The Anti-Christian Movement in Swatow" (1926)
Including "South China Diary" by Rev. A. S. Adams
Including "Early Mission History of the Swatow Region Brought Down to the Present for the American Baptist Mission" by Emanuel H. Giedt (1946)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series is divided into the following categories:

did.unittitle: ESH: autobiographical sketch of early years
ESH and LLH: passports, certificate of registration
ESH and LLH: newspaper clippings and memorabilia, including re. extended families
ESH: Related to YMCA work in Siberia
ESH and LLH: personal financial records
ESH and LLH: personal financial records
Chinese calling cards, wedding invitation, etc.
Primarily China missionaries and their children
Ceremony dedicating monument honoring ESH, with explanations

descriptivenote.p: Typhoon destruction of homes and boats
Two women and a man on a journey, Guangdong
People next to a waterfall, Guangdong
Missionary and girls on rocks, Shantou
Missionary preaching at a temple, Guangdong
People standing on a water pump, Guangdong
View from a mountain near Fenchow
Train between Shantou and Choa'an, Shantou
River, bridge, and buildings in the town, Chao'an
People boating down a river or canal, Jieyang
Man diving off a cliff, Guangdong
Golden Hill, cliff near river, Guangdong
Men pushing boats on river, Guangdong
Boy working a saw mill, Guangdong
Man working in a grain field, Guangdong
Bridge over the Grand Canal with boats, Hangzhou

did.unittitle: Including models of bound feet, straw slippers, small paintings
Oversize scrolls; Rubbing of granite memorial to ESH and others; Bamboo
